Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → DSA foundations and warm-ups → Arrays, hash maps, and two pointers

Lesson 1 of 3

Arrays, hash maps, and two pointers

Practice the fastest path from brute force to linear or near-linear array solutions.

30-45 min DSA flow DSA foundations and warm-ups 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

A large share of DSA screens begin here. Interviewers want to hear how you choose between indexing, frequency maps, sorting, and pointer movement.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array · Easy · Array3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing A large share of DSA screens begin here. Interviewers want to hear how you choose between indexing, frequency maps, sorting, and pointer movement. 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array · Easy · Array4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

A large share of DSA screens begin here. Interviewers want to hear how you choose between indexing, frequency maps, sorting, and pointer movement.

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Use this set to rehearse when to trade space for time with a hash map, when to sort first, and how to keep left/right pointer invariants stable through duplicates and boundary cases.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array, Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II, Rotate Array and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array · Easy · Array
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II · Easy · Array
  • Rotate Array · Easy · Array
  • Contains Duplicate · Easy · Array
  • Two Sum · Amazon · Arrays and Strings · Editor's choice: Frequently asked in an Amazon online assessment.
  • Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
4

Study section

Execution focus

Aim to state the brute-force baseline first, then replace it with the intended hash-map or pointer invariant without losing correctness around duplicates and empty inputs.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When does sorting unlock a two-pointer solution that beats a hash-map approach?
  • Which invariant keeps your left and right pointers from skipping the correct answer?
  • How would you explain the trade-off between O(n) space and O(n log n) sorting in an interview?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array

Easy Array

Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, remove the duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears only once. The relative order of the elements should be kept the same.

Since it is impossible to change the length of the array in some languages, you must instead have the result be placed in the first part of the array nums. More formally, if there are k elements after removing the duplicates, then the first k elements of nums should hold the final result. It does not matter what you leave beyond the first k elements.

Return k after placing the final result in the first k slots of nums.

Do not allocate extra space for another array. You must do this by modifying the input array in-place with O(1) extra memory.

Custom Judge:

The judge will test your solution with the following code:

int[] nums = [...]; // Input array
int[] expectedNums = [...]; // The expected answer with correct length

int k = removeDuplicates(nums); // Calls your implementation

assert k == expectedNums.length;
for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
    assert nums[i] == expectedNums[i];
}

If all assertions pass, then your solution will be accepted.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,1,2]
Output: 2, nums = [1,2,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 2, with the first two elements of nums being 1 and 2 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

Example 2:

Input: nums = [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
Output: 5, nums = [0,1,2,3,4,_,_,_,_,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 5, with the first five elements of nums being 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

 

Constraints:

  • 0 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104
  • -100 <= nums[i] <= 100
  • nums is sorted in non-decreasing order.

Hints

  • In this problem, the key point to focus on is the input array being sorted. As far as duplicate elements are concerned, what is their positioning in the array when the given array is sorted? Look at the image above for the answer. If we know the position of one of the elements, do we also know the positioning of all the duplicate elements? <br> <img src="https://assets.leetcode.com/uploads/2019/10/20/hint_rem_dup.png" width="500"/>
  • We need to modify the array in-place and the size of the final array would potentially be smaller than the size of the input array. So, we ought to use a two-pointer approach here. One, that would keep track of the current element in the original array and another one for just the unique elements.
  • Essentially, once an element is encountered, you simply need to <b>bypass</b> its duplicates and move on to the next unique element.

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 3 lines 8 words 0.1 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.
